import { Meta, ColorPalette, ColorItem } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
 
<Meta title="Corporate/Colors/Introduction" />
 

# Introduction to Colors
In order to create a clear recognition of the FSO graphics, the color palette was completely revised. In addition, it was ensured that all colors are barrier-free and comply with the [Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/).

The colors of a graphic are primarily intended to help the reader distinguish (dissociate) or connect (associate) things. However, the graphic creator must always try to use colors in a way that supports the message and readability. The same applies to the legend - it should be as logical and easy to read as possible.

The color should be used with care and as a complement to other visual variables such as position, shape or size. It is therefore recommended to use colors in the FSO's visualisations with moderation in order to maintain neutrality and objectivity. 

In order to make the application of colors easier, the new color palettes are divided into the following groups :
- [Qualitative colors](?path=/docs/corporate-colors-qualitative-colors--docs)
- [Sequential colors](?path=/docs/corporate-colors-sequential-colors--docs)
- [Diverging colors](?path=/docs/corporate-colors-diverging-colors--docs)
- [Political parties colors](?path=/docs/corporate-colors-political-parties-colors--docs)
- [Corporate colors](?path=/docs/corporate-colors-corporate-colors--docs)
- [Themes colors](?path=/docs/corporate-colors-themes-colors--docs)

visualisation-types-diagrams-area-chart-.content:
import { Meta, Canvas } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { moduleMetadata } from '@storybook/angular';
import * as chartStories from './areaChart.stories'


<Meta title="Visualisation Types/Diagrams/Area Chart/Simple Area Chart"/>

# Area Chart

## Table of contents
- [Description](#description)
- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)
- [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts)

## Description
An area chart is used to visualize changes to a metric value over time. Because it has only one metric, it serves the same function as a simple line chart. An area chart is distinguished from a line chart by the addition of shading between lines and a baseline, like in a bar chart.
<Canvas of={chartStories.Description} />
## Anatomy

## Chart variations
### Area chart with breaks
<Canvas of={chartStories.breaks} />
### Area chart with diverging areas
A Diverging Area Chart will present a standard area chart but with a diverging display of the data around a Base-line or Zero-line. It is used to compare values and to highlight 2 directions in the data over a single dimension. It should be used when the data includes negative and positive values. A diverging area chart however will have a zero-line or base-line with diverging areas on each side, which can in turn be highlighted by diverging colors. The shaded area of these charts allows a balance to be shown – either against a baseline or between two series. 
<Canvas of={chartStories.diverging} />
## Do's and don'ts
- Time intervals should be equal
- Avoid too complex data

visualisation-types-diagrams-area-chart-.name: Docs
visualisation-types-diagrams-area-chart-.title: Visualisation Types/Diagrams/Area Chart/Stacked Area Chart
visualisation-types-diagrams-area-chart-.content:
import { Meta, Canvas } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { moduleMetadata } from '@storybook/angular';
import * as chartStories from './stackedAreaChart.stories'


<Meta title="Visualisation Types/Diagrams/Area Chart/Stacked Area Chart"/>

# Stacked Area Chart

## Table of contents
- [Description](#description)
- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)
- [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts)

## Description
A stacked Area Chart shows the evolution of the variable along the X-axis, but also its relationship with the other variables and with the total (along the Y-axis). Each variable is represented by an individual, colored area.
The variables are stacked upon each other. In other words, each variable is stacked upon the previous in such a way that the previous serves as base-line to the next. The area between the X-axis and the top variable represents the total of all the variables.

This allows to check on the same figure the evolution of both the total of a numeric variable, and the importance of each group. 

Use the stacked area chart to show trends over time among related items.
<Canvas of={chartStories.Description} />
## Anatomy

## Chart variations
### Stacked area chart with breaks
<Canvas of={chartStories.breaks} />
## Do's and don'ts
- Y-axis should start at zero
- Exception made of the first line (closest to the X-axis), the values represented are added on top of the previous category, this means that the exact value can’t be read on the graph but needs to be calculated. What is shown is the relative importance of each group and the total of the stacked variables.
- Negative values can’t be treated
- Time intervals should be equal

import { moduleMetadata } from '@storybook/angular';
import * as chartStories from './arrowChart.stories'


<Meta title="Visualisation Types/Diagrams/Arrow Chart"/>

# Arrow Chart

## Table of contents
- [Description](#description)
- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)
- [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts)

## Description
The arrow chart is a specialised form of the connected dot plot (dumbbell plot). While a dumbbell plot treats both endpoints symmetrically, the arrow chart introduces directionality: an arrowhead at the end point unambiguously signals whether a value has increased or decreased, and by how much. This makes it particularly effective for before/after comparisons, temporal evolution, or any transition between two states.

Categories are displayed along the vertical axis; the horizontal axis carries the shared quantitative scale. Each row is one category, represented by a single arrow spanning from the start value to the end value. Colour and arrowhead direction encode whether the change is positive or negative.
<Canvas of={chartStories.Description} />
## Anatomy

## Chart variations
### Arrow chart with groups

visualisation-types-diagrams-bar-chart-b.title: Visualisation Types/Diagrams/Bar Chart/Bar Chart
visualisation-types-diagrams-bar-chart-b.content: import { Meta, Canvas } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks"; import { moduleMetadata } from '@storybook/angular'; import * as chartStories from './barChart.stories' # Bar Chart ## Table of contents - [Description](#description) - [Anatomy](#anatomy) - [Chart variations](#chart-variations) - [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts) ## Description A Bar Chart is similar to a Column Chart, both show comparisons among categories. The only difference is in the horizontal or vertical layout, by a simple inversion of the axes. The Bar Chart will have horizontal bars whereas the Column Chart will have vertical columns. The horizontal layout is the best choice for long text labels and trends, whereas the vertical layout is the best choice for short labels and trends. In a Bar Chart, the Y-axis represents the categories being compared while the X-axis represents a measured value. The Y-axis will generally be set with a categorical ordinal scale. The length of the bars is proportional to the values displayed. ## Anatomy ## Chart variations ### Bar chart with a confidence interval The lower and upper margin of the confidence interval is shown by an overlay. Usually we use the 95%-margin which has to be mentioned in the legend. In charts with only one colour, the hatched area is two steps darker than the bar chart. If there are multiple colours or different shades of the base colour, we use black #333333 instead. ### Bar chart with an overlay (mark?) ### Bar chart in groups ### Bar chart with diverging bars ## Do's and don'ts ### Axis starts at 0 Diagrams must always start at 0 otherwise the perception is distorted (unless the diagram represents an index, in which case the 100 value is mandatory). Deviations are allowed if the interruption is clearly marked. ### Horizontal layout All charts with long labels, have to be rotated by 90° to be compatible with small screens (mobile). ### Sort unordered categories by metric Appropriate sorting, which is almost always based on a metric, is important to effectively convey information in a bar chart and increase readability.

import { Meta, Canvas } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { moduleMetadata } from '@storybook/angular';
import * as chartStories from './groupedBarChart.stories'


<Meta title="Visualisation Types/Diagrams/Bar Chart/Grouped Bar Chart"/>

# Grouped Bar Chart

## Table of contents
- [Description](#description)
- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)
- [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts)

## Description
A Grouped Bar Charts is used when two or more data sets are displayed side-by-side and grouped together under the same categories. It is a standard way to compare the size of things, and it allows to compare multiple series. However, it can become tricky to read with more than 2 series. The horizontal layout is the best choice for long text labels and trends. Grouped bar charts can group the categories on the same axis.
<Canvas of={chartStories.Description} />
## Anatomy

## Chart variations
### Grouped bar chart with groups
<Canvas of={chartStories.groups} />
### Grouped bar chart with confidence interval
<Canvas of={chartStories.confidence} />
### Grouped bar chart with overlay
<Canvas of={chartStories.overlay} />
## Do's and don'ts
### Axis starts at 0
Diagrams must always start at 0 otherwise the perception is distorted (unless the diagram represents an index, in which case the 100 value is mandatory). Deviations are allowed if the interruption is clearly marked. 
### Horizontal layout
All charts with long labels, have to be rotated by 90° to be compatible with small screens (mobile). 
### Sort unordered categories by metric
Appropriate sorting, which is almost always based on a metric, is important to effectively convey information in a bar chart and increase readability.

<Meta title="Visualisation Types/Diagrams/Bar Chart/Stacked Bar Chart"/>

# Stacked Bar Chart

## Table of contents
- [Description](#description)
- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)
- [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts)

## Description
A Stacked Bar Chart is a simple way of showing part-to-whole relationships but can be difficult to read with more than a few components. The size of the components is more effective to read in a stacked bar chart than in a pie chart because it is a surface comparison. A stacked bar chart uses color differences to show several dimensions. To do this, each bar is split into a set of dimension items, distinguished by color, while maintaining the total bar length. A Stacked Bar chart is presented with horizontal bars. A horizontal layout is the best choice for long text labels and trends, whereas a vertical layout is the best choice for short labels and trends. 
<Canvas of={chartStories.Description2} />
## Anatomy

## Chart variations
### Stacked bar chart in groups
<Canvas of={chartStories.groups} />
## Do's and don'ts
### Axis starts at 0
Diagrams must always start at 0 otherwise the perception is distorted (unless the diagram represents an index, in which case the 100 value is mandatory). Deviations are allowed if the interruption is clearly marked. 
### Horizontal layout
All charts with long labels, have to be rotated by 90° to be compatible with small screens (mobile). 
### Sort unordered categories by metric
Appropriate sorting, which is almost always based on a metric, is important to effectively convey information in a bar chart and increase readability.

# Boxplot
## Table of contents
- [Description](#description)
- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)
- [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts)

## Description
Boxplot charts are used to summarize multiple distributions of quantitative data. It displays a central rectangle which extends from the 25th quantile (Q25) to the 75th quantile (Q75) and which is intersected by a line representing the median (Q50 or 50th quantile). This median line displays the value for which half of the data is below and the other half is above. The lines outside the rectangle are called whiskers and generally represent the 10th and 90th quantile (Q10 and Q90). Outliers may be plotted as individual points. The spacings between the different parts of the box indicate the degree of dispersion (spread) and skewness (asymmetry) in the data, and show outliers.

# Column Chart
## Table of contents

- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)

## Description
A Column Chart is similar to a Bar Chart, both show comparisons among categories. The only difference is in the horizontal or vertical layout, by a simple inversion of the axes. The Bar Chart will have horizontal bars whereas the Column Chart will have vertical columns. The vertical layout is the best choice for short labels and trends (years), whereas the horizontal layout is the best choice for long text labels and trends.

In a Column Chart, the X-axis represents the categories being compared, and the Y-axis represents a measured value. The X-axis will generally be set with a categorical ordinal scale. The height of the columns is proportional to the values displayed.

## Description
A stacked line column chart is a combination of a stacked column chart with a line chart. The stacked columns show the composition of a total value at a certain point in time and the overlaid line gives insight into a related trend, scenario or median value.

## Chart variations

## Do's and don'ts

- Do not use if the message to be communicated is about the changes of a particular segment. This type of chart makes it difficult to track how each segment of the columns evolves.
- If the data points are irregular, the line can suggest false trends.

## Description
A radar chart (also known as spider charts, polar charts, web charts, or star plots) presents a way to visualise multivariate data. It is used to plot one group of values over various shared variables. 
Each shared quantitative variable is represented by an axis starting at the center. All axes are radial. equally spaced, and of the same scale. The data is diusplayed on each axis and the connection of all data points forms a polygon.

## Anatomy

## Chart variations

## Do's and don'ts
- Make sure the variables are organised in a way that makes sense to the reader
- Opposite rays are not contrasting
- The position of each category does not convey any particular information

import { Meta, Canvas } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

import * as chartStories from './bubbleChart.stories'


<Meta title="Visualisation Types/Diagrams/Scatterplot/Bubble Chart"/>

# Bubble Chart
## Table of contents
- [Description](#description)
- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)
- [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts)

## Description
A Bubble Chart is used to look at the relationships between three numerical variables. The first and second variable will be plotted in function of values along the X and Y-axes as a scatterplot. The third variable will determine the size of the points, and therefore their respective weight.
It is important that the value of the third variable corresponds to the area of the bubble and not its diameter. In order to avoid hiding the small circles with the overlaying of big circles, it is mandatory to plot the smallest circles in front of the larger ones.
<Canvas of={chartStories.description} />
## Anatomy

## Chart variations
### Categorical Bubble Chart
<Canvas of={chartStories.categorical} />
## Do's and don'ts
- Superimposition treatment needs attention, small points in front of big points, to avoid loss of information
- The size/weight variable should be represented by the area of point and not the diameter
- The radius of the symbols is not the square root of the amount. Flannery correction should be applied
- Axes should start at zero
- Labels are mandatory
- Optional labels should not be superimposed
- Avoid too many superimposed elements

## Description
A slope chart is a line chart that represents a variable at two different points in time.
The time is shown horizontally, while the values are placed vertically. The evolution is represented by the slope.
A parallel coordinate chart is an extension of the slope chart with more than two different time points.

# Hierarchical Table
## Table of contents
- [Description](#description)
- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)
- [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts)

## Description
A hierarchical table is used in order to showcase structured data on multiple levels (p.ex: canton > district > municipality). It is helpful to analyse the repartition or organisation of a phenomena in relation to its structure.

Lines are used to represent observations, organized in a hierarchical manner. Columns are assigned a variable each.

## Anatomy

## Chart variations

### Hierarchical Table with total

### Hierarchical Table with highlight

## Do's and don'ts
- Not too many columns (* MH : how many maximum*)
- Not too many hierarchical levels (* MH : how many maximum*)
- One subject per table
- No cell fusion to guarantee machine readability

# Table with a visualisation
## Table of contents
- [Description](#description)
- [Anatomy](#anatomy)
- [Chart variations](#chart-variations)
- [Do's and don'ts](#dos-and-donts)

## Description
A table with a visualisation is a hybrid table containing numerical data and graphical elements in the same grid. It is used in order to put emphasis on certain trends, comparisons or rankings by integrating mini-graphs (bars, sparklines or icons) in the cells of a table.

Lines are used to represent observations, organized in a hierarchical manner. Columns are assigned a numerical variable or visual elements, if they add something to the readability, each.

### Table with columns

### Table with icons

- Not too many columns (* MH : how many maximum*)
- One subject per table
- No cell fusion to guarantee machine readability

Prefix the change with one of these keywords:

- Added: for new features.
- Breaking: for higher visibility of breaking changes
- Changed: for changes in existing functionality.
- Deprecated: for soon-to-be removed features.
- Removed: for now removed features.
- Fixed: for any bug fixes.
